column5: Thomas Hart Benton, born Neosho M) 1889, died Kansas City, MA 1975.  Achelous and Hercules, 1947.  tempera and oil on canvas mounted on plywood.  "Benton saw the legend as a parable of his beloved Midwest. The Army Corps of Engineers had begun efforts to control the Missouri River, and Benton imagined a future when the waterway was tamed and the earth swelled with robust harvests."  Also may reflect Marshall plan, as American agriculture saved Europe, and legendary stubbornness of people of Missouri, like Hercules, "tough and strong," with "a reputation for doing what he thought was right.".

column5: Synagogue, Ostia.  Main hall.  Discovered in 1961 at the edge of the town, near the ancient coastline, it Dates to the fourth century CE, though the excavations revealed two earlier phases of the building, the first of which Dates to the middle of the first century CE.   In its original construction, the synagogue consisted of a main hall with benches along three walls; a propyleum or monumental gateway with four marble columns; and a triclineum or dining room with three couches. A well with attached basin rested outside the entryway.  The main entrance of the synagogue faces the southeast, towards Jerusalem.

column5: Porticus of Octavia, Rome.  built ostensibly by Octavia, the sister of Augustus (Fest. 178; Ov. AA i.69), but really by Augustus and dedicated in the name of Octavia (Suet. Aug. 29; Cass. Dio xlix.43; Liv. Ep. 138) at some time after 27 B.C.  Besides the temples, there were within the enclosure a biblioteca erected by Octavia in memory of the youthful Marcellus (Suet. de gramm. 21; Plut. Marc. 30), a curia Octaviae (Plin. NH xxxvi.28), and a schola (ib. xxxv.114) or scholae (ib. xxxvi.22).  Whether these were different parts of one building, or entirely different structures, is uncertain. column5: Amphitheater at Trier.  View from above.  About 100 A.D.  On the outskirts of the town.  It was richly decorated during the following centuries.  Its western half held the imperial box and seats reserved for high ranking civil servants.  Its main entrances, to the north and south, originally featured magnificent facades.   The arena had room for roughly 20000 spectators.  In the 4th and 5th centuries the Amphitheater was at the same time a town gateway, so that the north entrance lay inside and the south entrance outside the town walls.

column5: Imperial Thermae, Trier.  Late Antique Baths, view from across a modern street.  First half of the fourth century.  The hot-water baths (caldarium), a hall containing a huge semicircular pool on the long east wall of the rectangular building, and two smaller pools along the shorter wall are the best preserved part of the Thermae.  The network of corridors in the building's basement were necessary for the maintenance of the heating system and for the drainage of the waste-water from the baths, while at the same time providing access to the stair towers and light shafts.

column5: Porta Nigra.  Trier. Close up of one of the two towers flanking the enormous gate.  This enormous gate was built of the distinctive light sandstone, by placing one row of huge blocks on top of another, and then joining them with iron clamps without using any mortar. 2nd century A.D.  Its height is 30 m and its depth 22 m, while its front measures 36 m in length. It has 2 carriage ways, each 7 m high, leading through a windowless ground floor. There are 144 windows total with Roman arches on the first and second floors at the front, and looking across the inner court, provided with portcullis and secured by heavily strengthened gates. The two towers flanking the Porta Nigra, while built flush with the rest of the walls inside the courtyard, jutted outwards in massive curves at the front, where they faced the open fields, and were linked with the high walls (7 m) surrounding the town, covering an area of 28500 acres.

column3: Gilded Terracotta Relief From Wooden Sarcophagi Collection
column4: Gilded Terracotta Relief From Wooden Sarcophagi Collection
column5:
The gilded terracotta reliefs with motifs from the world of Dionysus served as decoration for wooden coffins. Maenads, the god's female companions, and two young men lie together in pairs. Some hold musical instruments or sacrificial objects. As the plants suggest, all are situated in an idealized landscape. Here, no wild procession in honor of the revelry god takes place. The worshippers of Dionysus point to another aspect of the god.
In southern Italy and Thrace, Dionysus is also a god of the underworld. We look directly into the afterlife and see the god's blissful followers.
Animals also belong to the world of Dionysus. The panther is the god's quintessential symbolic animal. Griffins and lion-griffins represent wild, untamed nature, over which only the deity can command.

column3: Maenad and Panther Kylix Brygos Painter
column4: Maenad and Panther Kylix Brygos Painter
column5: No other depiction of a Maenad is as iconic as that of the Brygos Painter in Munich. She dances in a wild whirl with a snake in her hair and a panther in her hand. Filled with divine inspiration, she is no longer frightened by the dangers of life. The dark circular miscolouring in the middle has been caused by another kylix standing on top of this kylix during firing in the kiln.

column3: Symposium Music Lesson Hydria Side View
column4: Symposium Music Lesson Hydria Side View
column5: 2 hetairai reclining at symposion, playing kottabos; inscriptions - belly: music lesson: standing bearded man (paidagogos), seated youth with lyre, standing boy, seated bearded teacher with lyre. The main image shows a music student, to whom the painter Phintias has added the name of his colleague Euthymides. On the left, Demetrius listens to the music; behind him is the inscription "as he lives and breathes." A boy stands between the student and the teacher. On the shoulder of the vessel, two courtesans are playing the "kottabo game," in which the aim is to hurl the last drop of wine at a target. The courtesan with the headdress calls out: "This (drop) is for you." Beside her is written: "To the handsome Euthymides.".

column3: the Bronze Drum From the Idaean Cave
column4: the Bronze Drum From the Idaean Cave
column5: Bronze tympanon / shield that was found in the Ideaen cave, thought to be the site of Zeus' upbringing in Greek mythology. The tympanon depicts strong Eastern and Assyrian influence. The tympanon scene is interpreted as the myth of Zeus and the Curetes or Corybantes, where daemons beat drums to cover the crying of the infant Zeus, whom his mother Rhea had hidden in the Idaean Cave from his father Cronus, who devoured his own children.

column3: Hymn to Dictaean Zeus Or "Hymn of the Curetes"
column4: Hymn to Dictaean Zeus Or "Hymn of the Curetes"
column5: Fragments of a hymn possibly to the Cretan-born Zeus, inviting him to descend down from Mount Dikti and celebrate. Text of inscription was sung annually by men of the region, in addition to plenty of dancing and other festivities. Heraklion indicates that an inexperienced artisan had to re-carve this hymn, which is why there is some crude letter spacing.

column5: Stone rhyton with ritual scene in relief. It depicts a procession of men carrying agricultural implements for harvesting and winnowing, walking and singing to the rhythm of a sistrum. At the head of the procession is a man holding a staff who is dressed differently to the rest, in a long garment. He is probably an official or a priest.

column3: Ivory Plectrum For Playing the Lyre
column4: Ivory Plectrum For Playing the Lyre
column5: Found in an-already looted tomb from Poggio Gallinaro, the surviving remnants of the plectra, among other objects, such as a war chariot, underscore the desceased's exceptional rank. The ivory plectra for playing the lyre were likley placed in the tomb to evoke the music that accompanied the pivotal moments of aristocratic life.
